fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the error monitoring adapter library for our Ruby web apps. Basically the idea is that right now every team is manually wiring exception handling into their endpoints and it's a mess — different teams produce different payloads, some are leaking user IPs and cookies when they shouldn't be, and we're missing traces on a bunch of routes. We want a single library that wraps both our generic rack-style apps and our routed Rails-style apps and handles all this automatically.

The library needs to parse our monitoring DSN format (similar to what we discussed in the infra meeting last quarter), figure out the real client IP from proxy headers the same way the login module does it, pull request correlation IDs from the environment, sanitize request snapshots based on whether we're allowed to send personal data, and attach all that context to outgoing events. It also needs to wrap rack apps to catch exceptions and do transaction tracing, and integrate with the Rails request cycle for route-aware error reporting including manual captures inside controllers.

One thing that's been biting us is the privacy rules around cookies, IP headers, and request bodies — we need those to follow the pii flag strictly. Also the transaction naming for Rails errors needs to use the controller#action format. Can someone spec out the implementation? We should follow the same pattern we used for the last integration adapter project.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: on the DSN side, we should break it out into project_id, public_key, secret_key, scheme, host, port, path, canonical, and envelope_endpoint. project_id is the last path segment, public_key is the username, secret_key is the password, path is everything before the project_id segment, and canonical is just the original DSN string untouched. The DSN format is http://{public_key}:{secret_key}@{host}:{port}/{path}/{project_id}. For envelope_endpoint, use {base_path}/api/{project_id}/envelope/ and keep the trailing slash. base_path is just the DSN path minus the project_id segment. So for /ingest/42, base_path=/ingest, so envelope_endpoint=/ingest/api/42/envelope/. Same example written end to end: http://12345:67890@telemetry.example.com:3000/ingest/42 yields envelope_endpoint=/ingest/api/42/envelope/

Also, on client IP selection, the intended behavior is to prefer an explicit real/client address field if present and valid. If that’s not there, parse the X-Forwarded-For chain and select the oldest valid non-local address, which is the rightmost-trusted / leftmost-original one. We should skip private/loopback addresses while doing that. If every candidate is local/private, then fall back to the direct remote_addr value. That’s the same basic expectation here too: select oldest valid non-local address from X-Forwarded-For chain, falling back to remote_addr.

For request IDs, read HTTP_X_REQUEST_ID from the environment map first, then fall back to a framework-provided request-id field. If neither exists, use an empty string. The output there should stay a single line in the form request_id={value}.

One other detail on exception output formatting: all host-language exception class names like RuntimeError and StandardError should be normalized to application_exception anywhere we print them in stdout fields, specifically error= and exception_category=. We still keep the actual exception text exactly as-is in exception_message=.

And just to tighten up the privacy behavior: when send_default_pii is false, Cookie headers stay out of the headers map, cookies field is empty, request body/data is omitted, IP-related headers like X-Forwarded-For and REMOTE_ADDR are excluded, and user_ip is empty. When send_default_pii is true, cookies are included as a parsed object, form data is parsed into a structured data object, X-Forwarded-For header is included, REMOTE_ADDR is added to env, and user_ip is set to the selected client IP.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def self.call(env); def capture_exception(exception); def capture_message(message)

trigger_keywords: which headers are included in snapshot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Headers prefixed with HTTP_ in the environment are normalized by stripping the HTTP_ prefix, converting underscores to hyphens, and applying title-case to each segment (e.g. HTTP_X_REQUEST_ID → X-Request-Id). HTTP_COOKIE is never included in the normalized headers map regardless of the PII setting. HTTP_VERSION and similar non-header env fields are excluded from the headers output but the env map retains SERVER_NAME and SERVER_PORT.

For raised exceptions: the wrapper catches the exception, records an event with transaction set to the request path and exception category normalized to application_exception, then re-raises the exception — so response_status and response_body are empty. For environment-exposed errors (rack.exception / rack.errors): the original HTTP response (status + body) is preserved and returned to the caller while the exception event is still captured. event_count=1 in both cases.

trigger_keywords: when is a transaction event recorded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A transaction event is only recorded when tracing is enabled (traces_sample_rate > 0 and the request is sampled). When recorded, event_type=transaction, trace_status=ok, trace_operation=rack.request, span_count=0 for a basic successful request. When tracing is disabled or sample rate is 0, no transaction event is emitted and event_count=0.

For unhandled server errors in the routed framework, the transaction name is formatted as ControllerName#action (e.g. HelloController#exception). The request_url in the captured event is the full absolute URL of the originating request (e.g. http://www.example.com/exception). For normal successful routes (200) and excluded client-error routes (400), no event is captured so transaction and request_url are empty strings.

Client-style HTTP errors (4xx, e.g. 400 Not Found) are excluded from event capture — they return the framework status code but produce no monitoring event (event_count=0, transaction empty, exception fields empty). Only unhandled server failures (5xx) produce a captured event.

Code inside a routed controller can call capture_message or capture_exception. Both calls automatically attach the active request URL (e.g. http://example.org/test) and any scoped tags set on the current hub/scope (e.g. {"new_tag": true}). For message capture, exception_category is empty. For handled exception capture, exception_category is normalized to application_exception. The message field is populated only for message captures.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same pattern we used for the last integration adapter project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The multi-file SOLID architecture described in start.md: separate src/ modules for DSN parsing, IP selection, request snapshots, rack middleware, Rails integration, and a CLI adapter entry point that translates JSON stdin commands to core method calls

The send_default_pii boolean controlling: cookie header inclusion, cookies field, request body/data field, X-Forwarded-For header, REMOTE_ADDR in env, and user_ip — as specified in features 4 and 5 test cases
Feature 8 Rails transaction naming: transaction field is set to ControllerName#action (e.g. HelloController#exception) for captured server errors, as shown in feature8_rails_request_capture.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the exact technical details on that. You should be able to find the specifics by looking at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — each feature has a corresponding JSON file with input/output examples that should clarify the expected behavior. If it's about the overall architecture, check the constraints section in start.md.

feature1_dsn_components.json.description: Parses a monitoring endpoint address into connection fields and the envelope submission path.

cases.expected_output:
project_id=42
public_key=12345
secret_key=67890
scheme=http
host=telemetry.example.com
port=3000
path=/ingest
canonical=http://12345:67890@telemetry.example.com:3000/ingest/42
envelope_endpoint=/ingest/api/42/envelope/

feature2_real_client_ip.json.description: Selects the oldest valid public client address from forwarding and direct address fields.

feature3_request_id.json.description: Reads a request correlation identifier from supported request environment fields.

feature4_http_request_snapshot.json.description: Builds a sanitized HTTP request snapshot with URL, method, query string, selected headers, environment metadata, cookies, and body data according to the personal-data setting.

cases.expected_output:
url=http://example.org/test
method=GET
query_string=
headers={"X-Request-Id":"12345678"}
env={"SERVER_NAME":"example.org","SERVER_PORT":"80"}
cookies=
data=

feature5_event_request_context.json.description: Applies HTTP request context to an outgoing monitoring event while honoring whether default personal data may be sent.

feature6_rack_exception_capture.json.description: Wraps a generic HTTP application and records exceptions exposed either by raising or by standard request-environment error fields.

cases.expected_output:
response_status=
response_body=
error=application_exception
event_count=1
captured_url=http://example.org/test
transaction=/test

feature7_rack_transaction_trace.json.description: Records an HTTP transaction trace only when tracing is enabled, including trace status, operation, and span count.

feature8_rails_request_capture.json.description: Integrates with a routed web framework request cycle and reports framework-visible response and event details for normal, excluded, and unhandled request outcomes.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
body=Hello World!
event_count=0
transaction=
request_url=
exception_category=
exception_message=

status=400
body=
event_count=0
transaction=
request_url=
exception_category=
exception_message=

status=500
body=
event_count=1
transaction=HelloController#exception
request_url=http://www.example.com/exception
exception_category=application_exception
exception_message=An unhandled exception!

feature9_rails_controller_capture.json.description: Allows code inside a routed controller request to capture either a message or handled exception together with request URL and scoped tags.
